<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Takaku-jinja (Oiso)]][[ja:高来神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Takaku-jinja (Oiso)]][[ja:高来神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Takaku-jinja (Oiso)]][[ja:高来神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11670255]] Wikidata name: Takaku Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Takaku Shrine |image = File:Takaku Shrine - Kanagawa- 2023 April 16 various 22 39 54 464000.jpeg |location = [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]][[:en:Naka District, Kanagawa|Naka District, Kanagawa]][[:en:Ōiso|Ōiso Town]] Koma 2-chome 9-47 ([[:en:〒|〒]]255-0001) |coordinates = {{Coord|35.322222|N|139.324583|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |deities = {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |established = During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]] |nickname = Takaku-san (Komasan) |location = {{ill|Oiso|WD=Q385023}} |deity = {{ill|Kamimusubi|WD=Q3275901}}; {{ill|Ninigi|WD=Q1056017}} }}
[[File:Takaku shrine - Oiso Kanagawa - 2023 June 3 various 16 04 25 116000.jpeg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach path (2023 June)]]
'''Takaku Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高来神社}}, also known as '''Koma Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗神社}}) is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Ōiso|Ōiso Town]], [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]], Japan. The shrine holds the status of an {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (former district shrine).
The name "Takaku" is said to originate from the arrival of settlers from [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]], an ancient kingdom on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}.<ref>Pronunciation from [[:en:Wu Chinese|Wu Chinese]], [[:en:Southern Min|Southern Min]], and [[:en:Cantonese|Cantonese]]: kau-le, kau-lâi (ko-lâi), ko-lòi, etc.</ref>
== History == The exact founding date is unknown, but records claim it was established during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].
The shrine was once located at the summit of {{ill|Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|ja|高麗山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|1=Mount Koma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10872479}}, where it was known as '''Koma Gongen Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗権現社}}). To its right was {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}, and to its left stood [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]]. Together, these were known as the '''Koma Sansha Gongen''' (Three Shrines of Koma).
The shrine is closely linked to the migration of [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] people led by {{ill|Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|ja|高麗若光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|1=Koma no Jakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8010424}}, who settled in [[:en:Sagami Province|Sagami Province]] after the fall of their kingdom. The shrine's festival, known as "Mifune Matsuri" (Boat Festival), includes a song referencing the "Protector of Koma" who became the guardian of {{ill|Ōiso Bay|ja|大磯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōiso Bay|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/isotabi/taiken_asobu/powerspot/takakukomayama.html 高来神社(高麗神社) ・ 高麗山] (Ōiso Town)</ref>
The shrine was also a center of samurai devotion. [[:en:Minamoto no Yoritomo|Minamoto no Yoritomo]] is said to have prayed here for the safe childbirth of his wife, [[:en:Hōjō Masako|Hōjō Masako]]. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] donated a small piece of land to the shrine.
The shrine stands in the former village of {{ill|Koma Village|ja|高麗村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma Village|1=Koma Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673304}}, later incorporated into [[:en:Ōiso|Ōiso Town]].
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}} * {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]] * [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]]
== Timeline == * Reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]: Claimed founding era * Reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined * 533 AD (Reign of [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]]): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined * 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}} * 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ([[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]) * 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine) * 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings
== Annual Festivals == * '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1 * '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3 * '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18 ** Includes a procession ([[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]]) from the upper shrine to the main shrine * '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18 ** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years
== Access == * [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|JR East]] [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)]]: From [[:en:Ōiso Station|Ōiso Station]], take the bus toward [[:en:Hiratsuka Station|Hiratsuka Station]] North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

== Timeline == * Reign of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]: Claimed founding era * Reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]]: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined * 533 AD (Reign of [[:en:Emperor Ankan|Emperor Ankan]]): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined * 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}} * 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ([[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]) * 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine) * 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings
== Annual Festivals == * '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1 * '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3 * '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18 ** Includes a procession ([[:en:Mikoshi|Mikoshi]]) from the upper shrine to the main shrine * '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18 ** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years
== Access == * [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|JR East]] [[:en:Tōkaidō Main Line|Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)]]: From [[:en:Ōiso Station|Ōiso Station]], take the bus toward [[:en:Hiratsuka Station|Hiratsuka Station]] North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11670255]]
Wikidata name: Takaku Shrine
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]
|name = Takaku Shrine
|image = File:Takaku Shrine - Kanagawa- 2023 April 16 various 22 39 54 464000.jpeg
|location = {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|Naka District, Kanagawa|en|Naka District, Kanagawa|WD=Q1202849|lt=Naka District, Kanagawa}}{{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}} Koma 2-chome 9-47 ([[:en:〒|〒]]255-0001)
|coordinates = {{Coord|35.322222|N|139.324583|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
|deities = {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}
|shrine_Rank = {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}
|established = During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}
|nickname = Takaku-san (Komasan)
|location = {{ill|Oiso|WD=Q385023}}
|deity = {{ill|Kamimusubi|WD=Q3275901}}; {{ill|Ninigi|WD=Q1056017}}
}}

[[File:Takaku shrine - Oiso Kanagawa - 2023 June 3 various 16 04 25 116000.jpeg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach path (2023 June)]]

'''Takaku Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高来神社}}, also known as '''Koma Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗神社}}) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine holds the status of an {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (former district shrine).

The name "Takaku" is said to originate from the arrival of settlers from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}}, an ancient kingdom on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}.<ref>Pronunciation from {{ill|Wu Chinese|en|Wu Chinese|WD=Q34290|lt=Wu Chinese}}, {{ill|Southern Min|en|Southern Min|WD=Q36495|lt=Southern Min}}, and {{ill|Cantonese|en|Cantonese|WD=Q9186|lt=Cantonese}}: kau-le, kau-lâi (ko-lâi), ko-lòi, etc.</ref>

== History ==
The exact founding date is unknown, but records claim it was established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}.

The shrine was once located at the summit of {{ill|Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|ja|高麗山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|1=Mount Koma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10872479}}, where it was known as '''Koma Gongen Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗権現社}}). To its right was {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}, and to its left stood {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}}. Together, these were known as the '''Koma Sansha Gongen''' (Three Shrines of Koma).

The shrine is closely linked to the migration of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}} people led by {{ill|Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|ja|高麗若光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|1=Koma no Jakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8010424}}, who settled in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province}} after the fall of their kingdom. The shrine's festival, known as "Mifune Matsuri" (Boat Festival), includes a song referencing the "Protector of Koma" who became the guardian of {{ill|Ōiso Bay|ja|大磯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōiso Bay|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/isotabi/taiken_asobu/powerspot/takakukomayama.html 高来神社(高麗神社) ・ 高麗山] (Ōiso Town)</ref>

The shrine was also a center of samurai devotion. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} is said to have prayed here for the safe childbirth of his wife, {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako}}. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} donated a small piece of land to the shrine.

The shrine stands in the former village of {{ill|Koma Village|ja|高麗村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma Village|1=Koma Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673304}}, later incorporated into {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}}.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}
* {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}
* {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}

== Timeline ==
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}: Claimed founding era
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined
* 533 AD (Reign of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|en|Emperor Ankan|WD=Q241765|lt=Emperor Ankan}}): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined
* 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}
* 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}}
* 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}})
* 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine)
* 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings

== Annual Festivals ==
* '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1  
* '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3  
* '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18  
** Includes a procession ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}}) from the upper shrine to the main shrine  
* '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18  
** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years  

== Access ==
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}} {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)}}: From {{ill|Ōiso Station|en|Ōiso Station|WD=Q865175|lt=Ōiso Station}}, take the bus toward {{ill|Hiratsuka Station|en|Hiratsuka Station|WD=Q372513|lt=Hiratsuka Station}} North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

The shrine stands in the former village of {{ill|Koma Village|ja|高麗村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma Village|1=Koma Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673304}}, later incorporated into {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}}.

* {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}
* {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}
* {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}

== Timeline ==
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}: Claimed founding era
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined
* 533 AD (Reign of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|en|Emperor Ankan|WD=Q241765|lt=Emperor Ankan}}): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined
* 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}
* 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}}
* 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}})
* 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine)
* 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings

== Annual Festivals ==
* '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1  
* '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3  
* '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18  
** Includes a procession ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}}) from the upper shrine to the main shrine  
* '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18  
** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years  

== Access ==
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}} {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)}}: From {{ill|Ōiso Station|en|Ōiso Station|WD=Q865175|lt=Ōiso Station}}, take the bus toward {{ill|Hiratsuka Station|en|Hiratsuka Station|WD=Q372513|lt=Hiratsuka Station}} North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

|location = {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|Naka District, Kanagawa|en|Naka District, Kanagawa|WD=Q1202849|lt=Naka District, Kanagawa}}{{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}} Koma 2-chome 9-47 ([[:en:〒|〒]]255-0001)
|coordinates = {{Coord|35.322222|N|139.324583|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
|deities = {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}

[[File:Takaku shrine - Oiso Kanagawa - 2023 June 3 various 16 04 25 116000.jpeg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach path (2023 June)]]

'''Takaku Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高来神社}}, also known as '''Koma Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗神社}}) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine holds the status of an {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (former district shrine).

The name "Takaku" is said to originate from the arrival of settlers from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}}, an ancient kingdom on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}.<ref>Pronunciation from {{ill|Wu Chinese|en|Wu Chinese|WD=Q34290|lt=Wu Chinese}}, {{ill|Southern Min|en|Southern Min|WD=Q36495|lt=Southern Min}}, and {{ill|Cantonese|en|Cantonese|WD=Q9186|lt=Cantonese}}: kau-le, kau-lâi (ko-lâi), ko-lòi, etc.</ref>

== History ==
The exact founding date is unknown, but records claim it was established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}.

The shrine was once located at the summit of {{ill|Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|ja|高麗山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|1=Mount Koma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10872479}}, where it was known as '''Koma Gongen Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗権現社}}). To its right was {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}, and to its left stood {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}}. Together, these were known as the '''Koma Sansha Gongen''' (Three Shrines of Koma).

The shrine is closely linked to the migration of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}} people led by {{ill|Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|ja|高麗若光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|1=Koma no Jakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8010424}}, who settled in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province}} after the fall of their kingdom. The shrine's festival, known as "Mifune Matsuri" (Boat Festival), includes a song referencing the "Protector of Koma" who became the guardian of {{ill|Ōiso Bay|ja|大磯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōiso Bay|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/isotabi/taiken_asobu/powerspot/takakukomayama.html 高来神社(高麗神社) ・ 高麗山] (Ōiso Town)</ref>

The shrine was also a center of samurai devotion. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} is said to have prayed here for the safe childbirth of his wife, {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako}}. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} donated a small piece of land to the shrine.

The shrine stands in the former village of {{ill|Koma Village|ja|高麗村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma Village|1=Koma Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673304}}, later incorporated into {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}}.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}
* {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}
* {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}

== Timeline ==
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}: Claimed founding era
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined
* 533 AD (Reign of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|en|Emperor Ankan|WD=Q241765|lt=Emperor Ankan}}): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined
* 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}
* 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}}
* 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}})
* 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine)
* 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings

== Annual Festivals ==
* '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1  
* '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3  
* '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18  
** Includes a procession ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}}) from the upper shrine to the main shrine  
* '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18  
** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years  

== Access ==
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}} {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)}}: From {{ill|Ōiso Station|en|Ōiso Station|WD=Q865175|lt=Ōiso Station}}, take the bus toward {{ill|Hiratsuka Station|en|Hiratsuka Station|WD=Q372513|lt=Hiratsuka Station}} North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

To its right was {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}, and to its left stood {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}}. Together, these were known as the '''Koma Sansha Gongen''' (Three Shrines of Koma).

The shrine is closely linked to the migration of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}} people led by {{ill|Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|ja|高麗若光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|1=Koma no Jakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8010424}}, who settled in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province}} after the fall of their kingdom. The shrine's festival, known as "Mifune Matsuri" (Boat Festival), includes a song referencing the "Protector of Koma" who became the guardian of {{ill|Ōiso Bay|ja|大磯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōiso Bay|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/isotabi/taiken_asobu/powerspot/takakukomayama.html 高来神社(高麗神社) ・ 高麗山] (Ōiso Town)</ref>

The shrine was also a center of samurai devotion. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} is said to have prayed here for the safe childbirth of his wife, {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako}}. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} donated a small piece of land to the shrine.

The shrine stands in the former village of {{ill|Koma Village|ja|高麗村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma Village|1=Koma Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673304}}, later incorporated into {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}}.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}
* {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}
* {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}

== Timeline ==
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}: Claimed founding era
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined
* 533 AD (Reign of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|en|Emperor Ankan|WD=Q241765|lt=Emperor Ankan}}): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined
* 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}
* 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}}
* 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}})
* 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine)
* 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings

== Annual Festivals ==
* '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1  
* '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3  
* '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18  
** Includes a procession ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}}) from the upper shrine to the main shrine  
* '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18  
** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years  

== Access ==
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}} {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)}}: From {{ill|Ōiso Station|en|Ōiso Station|WD=Q865175|lt=Ōiso Station}}, take the bus toward {{ill|Hiratsuka Station|en|Hiratsuka Station|WD=Q372513|lt=Hiratsuka Station}} North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

[[File:Takaku shrine - Oiso Kanagawa - 2023 June 3 various 16 04 25 116000.jpeg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach path (2023 June)]]

'''Takaku Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高来神社}}, also known as '''Koma Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗神社}}) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine holds the status of an {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (former district shrine).

The name "Takaku" is said to originate from the arrival of settlers from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}}, an ancient kingdom on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}.<ref>Pronunciation from {{ill|Wu Chinese|en|Wu Chinese|WD=Q34290|lt=Wu Chinese}}, {{ill|Southern Min|en|Southern Min|WD=Q36495|lt=Southern Min}}, and {{ill|Cantonese|en|Cantonese|WD=Q9186|lt=Cantonese}}: kau-le, kau-lâi (ko-lâi), ko-lòi, etc.</ref>

== History ==
The exact founding date is unknown, but records claim it was established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}.

The shrine was once located at the summit of {{ill|Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|ja|高麗山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|1=Mount Koma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10872479}}, where it was known as '''Koma Gongen Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗権現社}}). To its right was {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}, and to its left stood {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}}. Together, these were known as the '''Koma Sansha Gongen''' (Three Shrines of Koma).

The shrine is closely linked to the migration of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}} people led by {{ill|Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|ja|高麗若光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|1=Koma no Jakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8010424}}, who settled in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province}} after the fall of their kingdom. The shrine's festival, known as "Mifune Matsuri" (Boat Festival), includes a song referencing the "Protector of Koma" who became the guardian of {{ill|Ōiso Bay|ja|大磯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōiso Bay|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/isotabi/taiken_asobu/powerspot/takakukomayama.html 高来神社(高麗神社) ・ 高麗山] (Ōiso Town)</ref>

The shrine was also a center of samurai devotion. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} is said to have prayed here for the safe childbirth of his wife, {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako}}. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} donated a small piece of land to the shrine.

The shrine stands in the former village of {{ill|Koma Village|ja|高麗村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma Village|1=Koma Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673304}}, later incorporated into {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}}.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}
* {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}
* {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}

== Timeline ==
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}: Claimed founding era
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined
* 533 AD (Reign of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|en|Emperor Ankan|WD=Q241765|lt=Emperor Ankan}}): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined
* 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}
* 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}}
* 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}})
* 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine)
* 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings

== Annual Festivals ==
* '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1  
* '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3  
* '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18  
** Includes a procession ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}}) from the upper shrine to the main shrine  
* '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18  
** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years  

== Access ==
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}} {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)}}: From {{ill|Ōiso Station|en|Ōiso Station|WD=Q865175|lt=Ōiso Station}}, take the bus toward {{ill|Hiratsuka Station|en|Hiratsuka Station|WD=Q372513|lt=Hiratsuka Station}} North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

[[File:Takaku shrine - Oiso Kanagawa - 2023 June 3 various 16 04 25 116000.jpeg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach path (2023 June)]]

'''Takaku Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高来神社}}, also known as '''Koma Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗神社}}) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine holds the status of an {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (former district shrine).

The name "Takaku" is said to originate from the arrival of settlers from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}}, an ancient kingdom on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}.<ref>Pronunciation from {{ill|Wu Chinese|en|Wu Chinese|WD=Q34290|lt=Wu Chinese}}, {{ill|Southern Min|en|Southern Min|WD=Q36495|lt=Southern Min}}, and {{ill|Cantonese|en|Cantonese|WD=Q9186|lt=Cantonese}}: kau-le, kau-lâi (ko-lâi), ko-lòi, etc.</ref>

== History ==
The exact founding date is unknown, but records claim it was established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}.

The shrine was once located at the summit of {{ill|Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|ja|高麗山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|1=Mount Koma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10872479}}, where it was known as '''Koma Gongen Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗権現社}}). To its right was {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}, and to its left stood {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}}. Together, these were known as the '''Koma Sansha Gongen''' (Three Shrines of Koma).

The shrine is closely linked to the migration of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo}} people led by {{ill|Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|ja|高麗若光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|1=Koma no Jakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8010424}}, who settled in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province}} after the fall of their kingdom. The shrine's festival, known as "Mifune Matsuri" (Boat Festival), includes a song referencing the "Protector of Koma" who became the guardian of {{ill|Ōiso Bay|ja|大磯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōiso Bay|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/isotabi/taiken_asobu/powerspot/takakukomayama.html 高来神社(高麗神社) ・ 高麗山] (Ōiso Town)</ref>

The shrine was also a center of samurai devotion. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} is said to have prayed here for the safe childbirth of his wife, {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako}}. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}} donated a small piece of land to the shrine.

The shrine stands in the former village of {{ill|Koma Village|ja|高麗村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma Village|1=Koma Village|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673304}}, later incorporated into {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town}}.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}
* {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}
* {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}}

== Timeline ==
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}: Claimed founding era
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined
* 533 AD (Reign of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|en|Emperor Ankan|WD=Q241765|lt=Emperor Ankan}}): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined
* 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}
* 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}}
* 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}})
* 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine)
* 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings

== Annual Festivals ==
* '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1  
* '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3  
* '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18  
** Includes a procession ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi}}) from the upper shrine to the main shrine  
* '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18  
** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years  

== Access ==
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}} {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)}}: From {{ill|Ōiso Station|en|Ōiso Station|WD=Q865175|lt=Ōiso Station}}, take the bus toward {{ill|Hiratsuka Station|en|Hiratsuka Station|WD=Q372513|lt=Hiratsuka Station}} North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

[[File:Takaku shrine - Oiso Kanagawa - 2023 June 3 various 16 04 25 116000.jpeg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach path (2023 June)]]

'''Takaku Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高来神社}}, also known as '''Koma Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗神社}}) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town|1=Ōiso}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine holds the status of an {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (former district shrine).

The name "Takaku" is said to originate from the arrival of settlers from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}}, an ancient kingdom on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}.<ref>Pronunciation from {{ill|Wu Chinese|en|Wu Chinese|WD=Q34290|lt=Wu Chinese|1=Wu Chinese}}, {{ill|Southern Min|en|Southern Min|WD=Q36495|lt=Southern Min|1=Southern Min}}, and {{ill|Cantonese|en|Cantonese|WD=Q9186|lt=Cantonese|1=Cantonese}}: kau-le, kau-lâi (ko-lâi), ko-lòi, etc.</ref>

== History ==
The exact founding date is unknown, but records claim it was established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}.

The shrine was once located at the summit of {{ill|Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|ja|高麗山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|1=Mount Koma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10872479}}, where it was known as '''Koma Gongen Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗権現社}}). To its right was {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}, and to its left stood {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}. Together, these were known as the '''Koma Sansha Gongen''' (Three Shrines of Koma).

The shrine is closely linked to the migration of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} people led by {{ill|Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|ja|高麗若光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|1=Koma no Jakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8010424}}, who settled in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}} after the fall of their kingdom. The shrine's festival, known as "Mifune Matsuri" (Boat Festival), includes a song referencing the "Protector of Koma" who became the guardian of {{ill|Ōiso Bay|ja|大磯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōiso Bay|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/isotabi/taiken_asobu/powerspot/takakukomayama.html 高来神社(高麗神社) ・ 高麗山] (Ōiso Town)</ref>

The shrine was also a center of samurai devotion. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} is said to have prayed here for the safe childbirth of his wife, {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako|1=Hōjō Masako}}. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} donated a small piece of land to the shrine.

The shrine stands in the former village of {{ill|Koma Village|ja|高麗村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma Village|1=Q11673304|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673304}}, later incorporated into {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town|1=Ōiso}}.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}

* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}: Claimed founding era
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined

* 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}
* 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}}
* 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}})
* 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine)
* 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings

== Annual Festivals ==
* '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1  
* '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3  
* '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18  
** Includes a procession ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}}) from the upper shrine to the main shrine  
* '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18  
** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years  

== Access ==
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}}: From {{ill|Ōiso Station|en|Ōiso Station|WD=Q865175|lt=Ōiso Station|1=Ōiso Station}}, take the bus toward {{ill|Hiratsuka Station|en|Hiratsuka Station|WD=Q372513|lt=Hiratsuka Station|1=Hiratsuka Station}} North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

[[File:Takaku shrine - Oiso Kanagawa - 2023 June 3 various 16 04 25 116000.jpeg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach path (2023 June)]]

'''Takaku Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高来神社}}, also known as '''Koma Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗神社}}) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town|1=Ōiso}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine holds the status of an {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (former district shrine).

The name "Takaku" is said to originate from the arrival of settlers from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}}, an ancient kingdom on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}.<ref>Pronunciation from {{ill|Wu Chinese|en|Wu Chinese|WD=Q34290|lt=Wu Chinese|1=Wu Chinese}}, {{ill|Southern Min|en|Southern Min|WD=Q36495|lt=Southern Min|1=Southern Min}}, and {{ill|Cantonese|en|Cantonese|WD=Q9186|lt=Cantonese|1=Cantonese}}: kau-le, kau-lâi (ko-lâi), ko-lòi, etc.</ref>

== History ==
The exact founding date is unknown, but records claim it was established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}.

The shrine was once located at the summit of {{ill|Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|ja|高麗山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|1=Mount Koma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10872479}}, where it was known as '''Koma Gongen Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗権現社}}). To its right was {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}, and to its left stood {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}. Together, these were known as the '''Koma Sansha Gongen''' (Three Shrines of Koma).

The shrine is closely linked to the migration of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} people led by {{ill|Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|ja|高麗若光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|1=Koma no Jakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8010424}}, who settled in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}} after the fall of their kingdom. The shrine's festival, known as "Mifune Matsuri" (Boat Festival), includes a song referencing the "Protector of Koma" who became the guardian of {{ill|Ōiso Bay|ja|大磯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōiso Bay|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/isotabi/taiken_asobu/powerspot/takakukomayama.html 高来神社(高麗神社) ・ 高麗山] (Ōiso Town)</ref>

The shrine was also a center of samurai devotion. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} is said to have prayed here for the safe childbirth of his wife, {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako|1=Hōjō Masako}}. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} donated a small piece of land to the shrine.

The shrine stands in the former village of {{ill|Koma Village|ja|高麗村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma Village|1=Q11673304|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673304}}, later incorporated into {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town|1=Ōiso}}.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}
* {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}
* {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}

== Timeline ==
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}: Claimed founding era
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined
* 533 AD (Reign of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|en|Emperor Ankan|WD=Q241765|lt=Emperor Ankan|1=Emperor Ankan}}): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined
* 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}
* 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}}
* 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}})
* 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine)
* 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings

== Annual Festivals ==
* '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1  
* '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3  
* '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18  
** Includes a procession ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}}) from the upper shrine to the main shrine  
* '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18  
** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years  

== Access ==
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}}: From {{ill|Ōiso Station|en|Ōiso Station|WD=Q865175|lt=Ōiso Station|1=Ōiso Station}}, take the bus toward {{ill|Hiratsuka Station|en|Hiratsuka Station|WD=Q372513|lt=Hiratsuka Station|1=Hiratsuka Station}} North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

|coordinates = {{Coord|35.322222|N|139.324583|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
|deities = {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}

[[File:Takaku shrine - Oiso Kanagawa - 2023 June 3 various 16 04 25 116000.jpeg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach path (2023 June)]]

'''Takaku Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高来神社}}, also known as '''Koma Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗神社}}) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town|1=Ōiso}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine holds the status of an {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (former district shrine).

The name "Takaku" is said to originate from the arrival of settlers from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}}, an ancient kingdom on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}.<ref>Pronunciation from {{ill|Wu Chinese|en|Wu Chinese|WD=Q34290|lt=Wu Chinese|1=Wu Chinese}}, {{ill|Southern Min|en|Southern Min|WD=Q36495|lt=Southern Min|1=Southern Min}}, and {{ill|Cantonese|en|Cantonese|WD=Q9186|lt=Cantonese|1=Cantonese}}: kau-le, kau-lâi (ko-lâi), ko-lòi, etc.</ref>

== History ==
The exact founding date is unknown, but records claim it was established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}.

The shrine was once located at the summit of {{ill|Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|ja|高麗山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|1=Mount Koma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10872479}}, where it was known as '''Koma Gongen Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗権現社}}). To its right was {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}, and to its left stood {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}. Together, these were known as the '''Koma Sansha Gongen''' (Three Shrines of Koma).

The shrine is closely linked to the migration of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} people led by {{ill|Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|ja|高麗若光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|1=Koma no Jakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8010424}}, who settled in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}} after the fall of their kingdom. The shrine's festival, known as "Mifune Matsuri" (Boat Festival), includes a song referencing the "Protector of Koma" who became the guardian of {{ill|Ōiso Bay|ja|大磯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōiso Bay|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/isotabi/taiken_asobu/powerspot/takakukomayama.html 高来神社(高麗神社) ・ 高麗山] (Ōiso Town)</ref>

The shrine was also a center of samurai devotion. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} is said to have prayed here for the safe childbirth of his wife, {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako|1=Hōjō Masako}}. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} donated a small piece of land to the shrine.

The shrine stands in the former village of {{ill|Koma Village|ja|高麗村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma Village|1=Q11673304|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673304}}, later incorporated into {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town|1=Ōiso}}.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}
* {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}
* {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}

== Timeline ==
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}: Claimed founding era
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined
* 533 AD (Reign of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|en|Emperor Ankan|WD=Q241765|lt=Emperor Ankan|1=Emperor Ankan}}): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined
* 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}
* 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}}
* 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}})
* 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine)
* 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings

== Annual Festivals ==
* '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1  
* '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3  
* '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18  
** Includes a procession ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}}) from the upper shrine to the main shrine  
* '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18  
** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years  

== Access ==
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}}: From {{ill|Ōiso Station|en|Ōiso Station|WD=Q865175|lt=Ōiso Station|1=Ōiso Station}}, take the bus toward {{ill|Hiratsuka Station|en|Hiratsuka Station|WD=Q372513|lt=Hiratsuka Station|1=Hiratsuka Station}} North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

The name "Takaku" is said to originate from the arrival of settlers from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}}, an ancient kingdom on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}.<ref>Pronunciation from {{ill|Wu Chinese|en|Wu Chinese|WD=Q34290|lt=Wu Chinese|1=Wu Chinese}}, {{ill|Southern Min|en|Southern Min|WD=Q36495|lt=Southern Min|1=Southern Min}}, and {{ill|Cantonese|en|Cantonese|WD=Q9186|lt=Cantonese|1=Cantonese}}: kau-le, kau-lâi (ko-lâi), ko-lòi, etc.</ref>

The shrine was once located at the summit of {{ill|Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|ja|高麗山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|1=Mount Koma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10872479}}, where it was known as '''Koma Gongen Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗権現社}}). To its right was {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}, and to its left stood {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}. Together, these were known as the '''Koma Sansha Gongen''' (Three Shrines of Koma).

The shrine is closely linked to the migration of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} people led by {{ill|Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|ja|高麗若光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|1=Koma no Jakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8010424}}, who settled in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}} after the fall of their kingdom. The shrine's festival, known as "Mifune Matsuri" (Boat Festival), includes a song referencing the "Protector of Koma" who became the guardian of {{ill|Ōiso Bay|ja|大磯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōiso Bay|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/isotabi/taiken_asobu/powerspot/takakukomayama.html 高来神社(高麗神社) ・ 高麗山] (Ōiso Town)</ref>

The shrine was also a center of samurai devotion. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} is said to have prayed here for the safe childbirth of his wife, {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako|1=Hōjō Masako}}. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} donated a small piece of land to the shrine.

* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}: Claimed founding era
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined
* 533 AD (Reign of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|en|Emperor Ankan|WD=Q241765|lt=Emperor Ankan|1=Emperor Ankan}}): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined
* 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}
* 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}}
* 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}})
* 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine)
* 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings

== Annual Festivals ==
* '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1  
* '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3  
* '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18  
** Includes a procession ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}}) from the upper shrine to the main shrine  
* '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18  
** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years  

== Access ==
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}}: From {{ill|Ōiso Station|en|Ōiso Station|WD=Q865175|lt=Ōiso Station|1=Ōiso Station}}, take the bus toward {{ill|Hiratsuka Station|en|Hiratsuka Station|WD=Q372513|lt=Hiratsuka Station|1=Hiratsuka Station}} North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

|name = Takaku Shrine
|image = File:Takaku Shrine - Kanagawa- 2023 April 16 various 22 39 54 464000.jpeg
|location = {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|Naka District, Kanagawa|en|Naka District, Kanagawa|WD=Q1202849|lt=Naka District, Kanagawa|1=Naka District, Kanagawa}}{{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town|1=Ōiso}} Koma 2-chome 9-47 ([[:en:〒|〒]]255-0001)
|coordinates = {{Coord|35.322222|N|139.324583|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
|deities = {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}

}}

[[File:Takaku shrine - Oiso Kanagawa - 2023 June 3 various 16 04 25 116000.jpeg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach path (2023 June)]]

'''Takaku Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高来神社}}, also known as '''Koma Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗神社}}) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town|1=Ōiso}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine holds the status of an {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (former district shrine).

The name "Takaku" is said to originate from the arrival of settlers from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}}, an ancient kingdom on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}.<ref>Pronunciation from {{ill|Wu Chinese|en|Wu Chinese|WD=Q34290|lt=Wu Chinese|1=Wu Chinese}}, {{ill|Southern Min|en|Southern Min|WD=Q36495|lt=Southern Min|1=Southern Min}}, and {{ill|Cantonese|en|Cantonese|WD=Q9186|lt=Cantonese|1=Cantonese}}: kau-le, kau-lâi (ko-lâi), ko-lòi, etc.</ref>

== History ==
The exact founding date is unknown, but records claim it was established during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}.

The shrine was once located at the summit of {{ill|Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|ja|高麗山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Koma (Kanagawa)|1=Mount Koma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10872479}}, where it was known as '''Koma Gongen Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗権現社}}). To its right was {{ill|Hakusan Gongen|ja|白山権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Gongen|qq=}}, and to its left stood {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}. Together, these were known as the '''Koma Sansha Gongen''' (Three Shrines of Koma).

The shrine is closely linked to the migration of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} people led by {{ill|Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|ja|高麗若光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Jakko (Koma Wakaikō)|1=Koma no Jakkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8010424}}, who settled in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}} after the fall of their kingdom. The shrine's festival, known as "Mifune Matsuri" (Boat Festival), includes a song referencing the "Protector of Koma" who became the guardian of {{ill|Ōiso Bay|ja|大磯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōiso Bay|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://www.town.oiso.kanagawa.jp/isotabi/taiken_asobu/powerspot/takakukomayama.html 高来神社(高麗神社) ・ 高麗山] (Ōiso Town)</ref>

The shrine was also a center of samurai devotion. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} is said to have prayed here for the safe childbirth of his wife, {{ill|Hōjō Masako|en|Hōjō Masako|WD=Q463961|lt=Hōjō Masako|1=Hōjō Masako}}. During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} donated a small piece of land to the shrine.

The shrine stands in the former village of {{ill|Koma Village|ja|高麗村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koma Village|1=Q11673304|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11673304}}, later incorporated into {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town|1=Ōiso}}.

== Enshrined Deities ==
* {{ill|Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja|神皇産霊尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}
* {{ill|Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja|天津彦穂邇々伎尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninigi-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}
* {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}

== Timeline ==
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}: Claimed founding era
* Reign of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}: Kami-Musubi and Ninigi-no-Mikoto enshrined
* 533 AD (Reign of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|en|Emperor Ankan|WD=Q241765|lt=Emperor Ankan|1=Emperor Ankan}}): Empress Jingū and Emperor Ōjin enshrined
* 717 AD (Yōrō 1): Shrine becomes a Buddhist temple complex under {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}
* 1591 AD (Tenshō 19): Receives a land grant (100 koku) from the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|徳川幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 江戸幕府}}
* 1868 AD (Meiji 1): Becomes solely a Shinto shrine ({{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}})
* 1873 AD (Meiji 6): Ranked as {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (district shrine)
* 1897 AD (Meiji 30): Renamed to '''Takaku Shrine''' after the nearby historical district of {{ill|Takaku-gō|ja|高来郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaku-gō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
* 1907 AD (Meiji 40): Designated as a shrine for imperial offerings

== Annual Festivals ==
* '''New Year's Festival''' (元旦祭): January 1  
* '''Setsubun Festival''' (節分祭): February 3  
* '''Spring Festival''' (春季festival): Closest weekend to April 18  
** Includes a procession ({{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|WD=Q979940|lt=Mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}}) from the upper shrine to the main shrine  
* '''Summer Grand Festival''' (夏季大祭): Closest Sunday to July 18  
** Known as the "{{ill|Ofune Matsuri|ja|御船祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}}" (Boat Festival) on even-numbered years  

== Access ==
* {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Tōkaidō Main Line|en|Tōkaidō Main Line|WD=Q1190152|lt=Tōkaidō Main Line (JR East)|1=Tōkaidō Main Line}}: From {{ill|Ōiso Station|en|Ōiso Station|WD=Q865175|lt=Ōiso Station|1=Ōiso Station}}, take the bus toward {{ill|Hiratsuka Station|en|Hiratsuka Station|WD=Q372513|lt=Hiratsuka Station|1=Hiratsuka Station}} North Exit and alight at "Kesho-zaka" or "Hanamizu" bus stop (3 minutes on foot).

[[File:Takaku shrine - Oiso Kanagawa - 2023 June 3 various 16 04 25 116000.jpeg|thumb|250px|Torii and approach path (2023 June)]]

'''Takaku Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高来神社}}, also known as '''Koma Shrine''' ({{lang|ja|高麗神社}}) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Ōiso|en|Ōiso|WD=Q385023|lt=Ōiso Town|1=Ōiso}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine holds the status of an {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} (former district shrine).

The name "Takaku" is said to originate from the arrival of settlers from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}}, an ancient kingdom on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|WD=Q483134}}.<ref>Pronunciation from {{ill|Wu Chinese|en|Wu Chinese|WD=Q34290|lt=Wu Chinese|1=Wu Chinese}}, {{ill|Southern Min|en|Southern Min|WD=Q36495|lt=Southern Min|1=Southern Min}}, and {{ill|Cantonese|en|Cantonese|WD=Q9186|lt=Cantonese|1=Cantonese}}: kau-le, kau-lâi (ko-lâi), ko-lòi, etc.</ref>
